channel.title: Software on Owen Boberg
channel.description: Recent content in Software on Owen Boberg

item.title: odi-tools
item.description:
The One Degree Imager (ODI) is a relatively new instrument on the WIYN 3.5-meter telescope at the Kitt Peak National Observatory (KPNO). In its current configuration, the ODI focal plane is made up of 30 individual orthogonal transfer array (OTA) CCDs covering approximately a degree on the diagonal. The raw data taken on ODI are stored and reduced on the ODI Pipeline, Portal, and Archive (PPA) using a Python based pipeline called QuickReduce.

To cover the gaps between the 30 OTAs, as well as the gaps internal to each OTA, a single ODI field typically requires a 9 point dither pattern. This essentially results in 270 individual images that have to be combined into the final stacked image. Working with another graduate student at Indiana University, Bill Janesh, we developed a Python pipeline called odi-tools to reproject, align, scale, and stack ODI images. The image shown at the top of this this page is a portion of and ODI field stacked using odi-tools. The object in this image is the globular cluster M 53. By using the Gaia DR1, we are able to determine a very precise WCS solution for all of the OTAs that go into a stack images. This was crucial for being able to stack crowded fields like the one shown in M 53.

The code for odi-tools is completely open source and hosted on GitHub. There is also full Read The Docs style documentation available here.

item.title: Instrumentation
item.description:
APO 1-meter Telescope

Working with Dr. Thomas Harrison at my undergraduate institution, New Mexico State University (NMSU), I wrote the software to control a high speed 5-band photometer on the 1-meter NMSU telescope at the Apache Point Observatory. The software was written in LabView and provided an easy to use graphical user interface that controlled the data acquisition and telescope positioning. The image was taken from this link.

2.5-meter Sloan Telescope

During the summer before my graduate courses started, I undertook and additional software design project at NMSU with Dr. Jon Holtzman. My role was to create a program that would allow observers to use the Apache Point Observatory Galactic Evolution Experiment (APOGEE) spectrograph, located on the 2.5 meter Sloan telescope, from the adjacent NMSU 1-meter telescope. This allows observers to collect spectra of astronomical objects during bright time (near full moon) on a smaller aperture telescope, when the larger aperture Sloan telescope cannot be used. The software package I wrote for this project was written in Python.
